data.title: Whirlpool Is Transitioning From Major Headwinds To Major Tailwinds
data.description:
I have written a number of articles in Seeking Alpha over the years about turnarounds. It has been my most successful strategy, though finding good ones is difficult. I usually like to see signs of the turnaround in process before I invest. But sometimes, I don’t wait if strong catalysts are in place. That is the case with Whirlpool Corporation (WHR).

Companies with beaten-down stocks tend to be that way for one of five reasons: recession, industry decline, non-recurring issues, poor management, or cyclicality. Whirlpool’s stock is down for 2 of those reasons: non-recurring issues and cyclicality. It has been a double whammy. The non-recurring issue is tariffs, which Whirlpool was expected to benefit from and should become a significant tailwind shortly. But initially, it has been a major headwind. The cyclicality is in their largest markets, new and existing home sales. Whirlpool's stock was further hit by management decisions to cut the dividend in half last summer and a recent dilutive stock offering. Those should also be non-recurring.

In this article, I will detail a number of catalysts and tailwinds in addition to tariffs and an eventual recovery in the housing market. But make no mistake, those two alone could more than double the stock.

Background

Whirlpool is the largest manufacturer and seller of major appliances in North America. Its primary brands in the U.S. are Whirlpool, Jenn-Air, Maytag, KitchenAid, and Amana. It manufactures 80% of what it sells in the U.S. domestically. It also owns InSinkErator (under-sink food disposal), which has a dominant 70% market share in the U.S. and 31% globally.

InSinkErator was acquired in November 2022 for $3.0 billion. In April 2024, Whirlpool sold its European operations for a loss totaling $1.9 billion. After these transactions, Whirlpool was left with a highly leveraged balance sheet just as earnings were dropping from COVID-era highs.

In February 2026, Whirlpool issued $1 billion of new common and preferred stock. The preferred, which totaled $525 million, pays an 8.50% coupon. It automatically converts to common stock at between $69 and $81, depending on the common stock price in 2029, if not converted earlier. The total dilution to common stock is about 24%. Management did the transaction to reduce leverage and provide future flexibility.

The company is somewhat seasonal, with the strongest quarters being the third and fourth. In August 2025, Whirlpool cut its quarterly dividend amount from $1.75 to $0.90. The dividend currently yields 6.2%. The only insider activity in the past 3 months not involving restricted stock was an open market purchase of $198,800 of stock by Director Creed on March 3rd.

Financials

Financial results for the last 5 years, plus guidance for 2026 are shown below.

As shown above, Whirlpool had some of its strongest years ever in 2021 through 2023. Those years were at the top of the cycle and driven by Covid, when people focused on their homes. Before Covid, Whirlpool was up and down and was impacted by cheap imports.

Whirlpool was unable to take advantage of its peak earning years as tangible net worth declined into negative territory and interest-bearing debt increased. This was primarily due to a large loss on the sale of their European business, and massive goodwill and intangible assets were added when acquiring InSinkErator. Revenues declined over the past 5 years in part because the InSinkErator business has less revenue than the European business it replaced.

Revenues and earnings in 2025 were significantly impacted by tariffs. While the tariffs were supposed to help domestic manufacturers like Whirlpool in the short term, they were a big headwind for two reasons. Firstly, major competitors frontloaded massive inventory into the U.S. ahead of the tariffs fully taking effect. This created an oversupply of product, resulting in fewer sales for Whirlpool and lower prices. Tariffs also increased Whirlpool’s cost of components and some finished products by $300 million in 2025.

Guidance

The 2026 guidance was recently adjusted for the stock issuance dilution, and 2026 EPS was adjusted down from $7.00 to $6.00. The company does expect to save $37 million per year in interest expense. CFO Roxanne Warner on the conference call said they expect to pay down debt by $400 million in 2026. She has since said another $900 million will be paid down from the stock issuance proceeds. That should bring total interest-bearing debt down to around $5.2 billion. The guidance estimates 5% organic revenue growth.

The $6.00 2026 EPS guidance appears quite cautious to me. It also does not reflect improved pricing in the market as the tariffs fully start to impact competitors. Management has said they are not seeing a major improvement yet, so they appear to be conservative. It also reflects a normal tax rate in 2026 versus a few taxes paid in 2025.

Tailwinds

Whirlpool has a number of tailwinds in place or likely to emerge. These are listed below.

1. Tariffs

U.S. tariffs are a major factor for Whirlpool because North America is Whirlpool’s largest market, with over 65% of total sales, and the U.S. is the vast majority of that. Tariffs are likely to shift from a major headwind to an equally large tailwind during 2026. Whirlpool is the only major appliance manufacturer based in the U.S., so it is the only beneficiary in the industry. Whirlpool manufactures 80% of what it sells in the U.S., way more than any competitor. The benefit should be quite large once it kicks in.

As I mentioned earlier, many of their competitors frontloaded products into the U.S. to get ahead of the tariffs. Since tariffs didn’t go into full effect until later in the year, this continued well into the second half of the year. It created an oversupply of product, resulting in fewer sales for Whirlpool and lower prices. To make matters worse, tariffs also increased Whirlpool’s cost of components and some finished products by $300 million in 2025. The pricing pressure appears to have stabilized and is starting to move in Whirlpool’s favor, as indicated by CEO Bitzer’s comments in Whirlpool’s January 29, 2026, conference call.

“We observed a meaningful change in industry pricing and promotions after mid-December into the MLK holiday and the upcoming President's Day.”

Management also noted in the call that the oversupply of appliances has been depleted, and it is seeing reduced promotional intensity in the U.S.

Going forward, Whirlpool should have a major advantage over its competitors. They are paying tariffs currently of around 15% on the non-steel portion of their imports and 50% on the steel content. Whirlpool only pays tariffs on 20% of what it sells in the U.S. and sources 96% of its steel from the U.S. These 2 tariffs should give Whirlpool a huge cost advantage going forward. Management believes that the advantage will be 5-15%. At the current rate of tariffs, the advantage is at least 10%. Management believes that its competitors who dumped product in the U.S. are losing money and will be forced to adjust pricing upward. The full impact of tariffs hasn’t hit them yet.

The key here is that even if tariffs go away, Whirlpool will still benefit from not having to face competitors dumping product at low prices and paying $300 million for imports of components and finished goods. If tariffs stay, the dumping goes away, and Whirlpool has a big cost advantage over everyone. The swing from 2025 to 2027 should be quite large.

2. Housing Cycle

While the tariff situation is near-term, a housing cycle upturn is probably farther off. New and existing home purchases are a very large source of buyers for Whirlpool. Both markets have been at the bottom of the cycle in the U.S. since 2023 due to a major increase in home prices and a significant increase in long-term interest rates. There is currently a 4 million-home housing shortage in the U.S., according to realtor.com. Others estimate this shortage even higher. Many homeowners are holding back because they are paying much less than the current 6% market interest rate on their existing mortgages. If and when longer-term interest rates drop significantly, it will provide a major tailwind for Whirlpool. Take a look at 2021-2023 in the chart shown earlier for what that potential is. Earnings were 3-4x current earnings back then when rates were much lower. Covid also helped then, but this time, pent-up demand is as big as it has been in decades.

3. Cost Reductions

Management expects $150 million of cost reductions in 2026. This is on top of $200 million in 2025. The cuts in 2025 were overwhelmed by the tariff headwinds. Whirlpool recently announced 341 job cuts at its Amana, Iowa, plant. Whirlpool is using AI to improve factory efficiency. The biggest source of cuts is expected to come from vertical integration and automation in the factories. These reductions are likely to be higher in the second half of the year.

4. New Product Launches

CEO Bitzer, in the last conference call, stated that new product launches in 2025 were the most in a decade. They updated or initiated 30% of their products last year. That cost them in 2025 as they had to pay for floor space and ramp-ups. CEO Bitzer stated in the call,

“The new products that we launched in North America gained over 30% incremental flooring on a like-for-like basis.”

More floor space at retailers usually leads to higher sales.

5. Small Appliances Growth

Whirlpool’s small appliance segment is called SDA Global. It currently represents about 7% of sales and is growing much more rapidly organically than the rest of the company. CEO Bitzer stated the following about it in the last call.

“SDA Global continues to perform very well, achieving impressive net sales growth of approximately 10% year-over-year in the fourth quarter and approximately 9% on a full-year basis, driven by new product launches and strong direct-to-consumer business. Fourth quarter EBIT margins expanded 130 basis points year-over-year as a result of a favorable price mix. For the full year, the segment delivered a strong EBIT margin of 16% with 170 basis points of margin expansion year-over-year.”

6. Activist

Well-known activist David Tepper’s hedge fund, Appaloosa, LP, owns 4.9% of Whirlpool. On February 25, 2026, they sent a letter to the board indicating concern about the recent equity issuance, failure to take advantage of the tariffs, and management entrenchment. Appaloosa is large enough to enter a proxy contest or even try to take over Whirlpool. Whirlpool is only its tenth-largest holding.

7. Litigation

In November 2025, Whirlpool filed a complaint with the U.S. International Trade Commission against LG, Samsung, and GE (Haier), requesting an investigation into patent infringement related to the company’s low-profile microwave hood combination products.

Risks

As with all companies, there are a number of risks with Whirlpool stock.

1. Balance Sheet

The balance sheet, though improved, remains highly leveraged. The EBITDA to debt ratio after the expected debt paydown from the stock issuance will be just under 5x. Tangible net worth was negative $3.0 billion on December 31, 2025. The biggest issue here is a possible additional dividend cut if earnings don’t improve.

2. Recession

Whirlpool is a cyclical company that is susceptible to recessions. However, a recession may also lower long-term rates, which could significantly improve the housing industry, its largest customer base. Whirlpool may actually benefit from a recession if rates come down significantly.

3. Geographic Concentration

Whirlpool gets 65% of sales from North America, most of which is the U.S.

Valuation

Management at the March 2, 2026, Raymond James Conference stated that its midcycle EBIT expectation is 9%. Midcycle means a normal operating environment, which excludes the current cyclical lows and the prior COVID-era highs. After deducting interest at about $300 million a year (after paydowns from the stock issuance) and taxes at 20%, that leaves a profit margin of 5.7%. That profit margin on guided sales of $15.45 billion indicates a profit of $880 million, which is $12.35/share based on the current diluted shares outstanding. There is a good chance we will be at least mid-cycle in 2027 from a tariff reversal alone and assuming no improvement from the housing industry.

My valuations usually compare the subject company to its peers and then adjust for growth, leverage, and other factors. Finding peers was a bit difficult as there is no other U.S.-based major appliance manufacturer. The first 3 on the list below are U.S.-based large home goods manufacturers. The final three are an overseas direct peer (Electrolux), a domestic small appliance manufacturer (Hamilton Beach), and an overseas small appliance maker (SEB). I did not include Shark Ninja as it is growing too fast to be comparable and is not a direct competitor.

This peer group suffers from poor growth, resulting in well below market P/E ratios and prices to revenues. SEB SA’s growth in 2024 was primarily due to acquisitions. Its organic growth was near breakeven in 2025.

Whirlpool’s revenue growth has been below its peers, in part due to divestitures. But it is guiding for above-peer growth this year. Its profit margin has recently been below that of its peers, though it was well above in 2021 through 2023. Whirlpool is more leveraged than any peer shown, though at this point not concerningly so. Whirlpool is priced at half of its peers based on price to sales, but similar based on its P/E ratio. Based on the peers, I believe Whirlpool’s P/E Ratio of 11.3 is priced at the market. However, that assumes no catalysts. Whirlpool has way more catalysts going forward than any of its peers. In fact, the tariff reversal alone could move its earnings to mid-cycle, if not farther.

I previously estimated mid-cycle EPS at $12.35 based on management’s guidance of a mid-cycle EBIT margin of 9%. P/E ratios tend to contract somewhat as an industry moves upward in its cycle. Based on that, I believe a price target for mid-2027 is 10x the $12.35 EPS mid-cycle estimate. That equates to $123.50. That is more than double the closing price of $57.55 on March 10, 2026. If and when the housing market recovers, it could go much higher, perhaps another double.

Takeaway

Two items out of Whirlpool’s control (tariffs and a housing downturn) plus two they controlled (a dividend cut and dilutive secondary offering) have upset shareholders, resulting in a much lower stock price. The latter 2 are in the rearview mirror. Tariffs are poised to go from a major headwind to a major tailwind. The housing market, due to pent-up demand, will likely rally strongly if and when long-term interest rates come down.

This is an opportunity to buy a cyclical company at its trough and with catalysts in place for a major rebound. Whirlpool has significant earnings power as shown by EPS exceeding $16 during 2021-2023. Meanwhile, the balance sheet, though still leveraged, has been improved and shouldn’t be a concern for a while. I recommend a long position in Whirlpool, with an 18-month price target of $123.50. I don’t expect much recovery this quarter and perhaps next, but we should really start seeing it in the second half of this year. That also happens to be the seasonally stronger part of the year.

Another benefit is that Whirlpool may be one of the few countercyclical stocks right now, along with the homebuilders. Recessions or even economic weakness usually result in lower interest rates. Lower interest rates are needed to make housing more affordable.

data.title: This One Market Signal Could Change Everything For Private Credit
data.description:
The bullish thesis for private credit boiled down to two factors:

  1. It has historically achieved better returns than its high-yield peers, including leveraged loans and high-yield bonds (HYG).
  2. The private wealth channel was largely under-penetrated, and institutional investors still had further to allocate to the space.

Thus, alternative asset managers like Ares Management Corporation (ARES), Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO), Blackstone Inc. (BX), Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), and others are expected to continue to generate significant assets under management growth in the coming years.

Meanwhile, investors in their funds, as well as in publicly traded BDCs (BIZD) like Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX), Capital Southwest (CSWC), Ares Capital (ARCC), and others, could expect to continue to generate outsized total returns relative to other high-yielding debt sectors.

However, recently the market has been sending a loud and clear message, as backed up by several different metrics, that indicates that the growth expectations and potentially the total return expectations for the sector were overinflated. In this article, I will detail what this message is and share how I am allocating capital as a result.

Why Private Credit Became One Of The Market's Most Popular Income Strategies

The case for private credit, both from an asset manager and from a direct investor standpoint, makes a lot of sense. The fundamental qualities of private credit also make sense, especially many of its funds, given that they are primarily focused on senior secured loans that have a considerable equity cushion beneath them in the capital stack and are overwhelmingly to companies backed by private equity sponsors. Additionally, many of the loans have tangible assets, or at the very least recurring cash flows backing them, and many of the businesses are growing at a solid clip with strong interest coverage ratios.

Moreover, these private credit funds are managed by skilled investors who are very knowledgeable in the space and actively manage their portfolios, including underwriting all the way through workouts, in order to maximize recoveries and minimize losses over time. They also effectively diversify to enhance their risk-reward profiles, and on top of that, they tend to invest in floating-rate loans with fairly short durations that make them compelling diversification instruments for investors who are looking for income-generating alternatives to long-duration fixed-yield securities, whether that be high-yield bonds, preferred stocks (PFF), leveraged loans (BKLN), or even triple-net lease REITs (VNQ) like Realty Income Corporation (O).

The Early Warning Signs That Private Credit May Be Overheating

However, numerous tremors are showing up in the data that indicate that the golden age of private credit may not, in fact, be here. The earliest warning sign was that a lot of capital poured into the space at a much faster pace than deals were coming to market, largely due to the bullish projections for the sector and the compelling pitch that leading alternative asset managers were making to investors about the attractive risk-reward profile of private credit.

This led to spreads tightening, which is a basic law of economics whenever supply and demand shift relative to the previous equilibrium. This, in turn, led to reduced returns for investors as well as growing concerns that lending standards were slackening. This was made even worse by the fact that the boom in private credit led to a surge in new entrants into the space, including those that lacked sufficient expertise to underwrite loans prudently.

All this concern has since led the public markets to send a message that is loud and clear, both through publicly traded BDCs as well as through publicly traded alternative asset managers that are heavily concentrated in direct lending.

Why BDC Discounts To NAV Are Sending A Powerful Market Signal

On the public BDC side, we have seen valuations plunge with yawning price-to-NAV gaps, including in some of the most prestigious and well-run BDCs out there, such as ARCC, Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). These huge discounts to NAV have made some investors, including myself, scratch our heads when looking at what appear to be very solid underlying fundamentals that are well within historical norms, sound balance sheets, and attractive dividend yields.

It seems like the market is pricing in a private-credit apocalypse that has yet to show any symptoms of emerging. Of course, it could emerge, and as GBDC's CEO recently warned, these are not rosy times for private credit, and it is an increasingly challenged environment due to the supply-demand dynamics as well as uncertainties about disruptions to software, which several BDCs do have outsized exposure to.

However, I think the signal that is being sent is actually slightly different, and this is also being communicated through the stock prices of alternative asset managers, despite posting solid fundamentals and growth as well:

What is the signal? It is simply saying that the market is not ready for as much capital as these alternative asset managers were expecting it to be. Between the narrowing spreads and the headlines about retail fund withdrawals and companies like OWL, BX, and, most recently, BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), it appears that at least some retail investors still have the jitters about private credit's durability. Moreover, the compressing yields are signaling that the market is trying to ram too much capital into private credit that it's not yet ready for. This is also seen in the reduced price-to-NAV multiples on many BDCs.

The Critical Role Of Buybacks In Restoring Balance To The Sector

So how does this correct itself? I think one of the most obvious ways, besides the outflow of capital from some private BDCs and hopefully the education of retail investors and financial advisors about how these funds actually work and that they are not currently undergoing Armageddon, is that public BDCs, in particular those that are trading at huge discounts to NAV, may need to reinvest proceeds from repaid loans into paying down debt and buying back their stock at steep discounts to NAV.

The challenge of this actually coming into reality, though, is seen in FS KKR Capital Corp's (FSK) recent earnings call, where management was directly confronted by analysts about the attractiveness of buying back stock while it was trading at a near 50% discount to NAV and their hesitancy to commit to anything along those lines. The issue lies in the fact that the sponsors of these externally managed BDCs generate fees from assets under management, and these publicly traded BDC vehicles are very attractive as permanent capital vehicles. Therefore, buying back stock effectively reduces their AUM that they could otherwise earn fees on. This is why they often prefer to reinvest the proceeds from loan repayments into making new loans rather than buying back their own stock, even if the stock trades at a huge discount to NAV.

At the same time, it is refreshing to see funds like Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC), OBDC, Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), BXSL, and others all buy back stock and commit to further buybacks moving forward while their equities trade at meaningful discounts to NAV. This is the sort of movement that will be needed to bring balance to the BDC sector by removing some froth/excess capital chasing too few deals. In turn, it is signaling confidence to the market that some of these leading lenders do feel confident in the quality of their portfolios by doubling down on them through buybacks. If private credit is indeed as sound as the lenders are making it out to be, these buybacks should be no-brainers from a value perspective.

How I Am Allocating Capital In Private Credit Right Now

So how am I investing in the current environment?

Firstly, I am not fleeing from the private credit space as some are, given the negative headlines and uncertainties. Certainly, it appears that many BDCs and, even worse, alternative asset managers are falling knives right now. Thus, buying today can seem like an unnecessary risk, given that if current momentum holds, you're likely to see significant red in your portfolios in the coming weeks.

I disagree with the idea behind completely avoiding the sector, though, because, as a long-term investor, I am convinced that it is impossible to perfectly time the bottom. Moreover, I am not a technical investor who wants to see a recovery start before buying, as a price going up does not make me want to buy it more, but rather less.

Instead, I am gradually and prudently allocating capital to the sector, both in the alternative asset management space and in the BDC space, where I see value and quality intersect. In particular, I am looking for alternative asset managers whose insiders are buying their own stock to double down on it, as well as the company itself buying back stock.

Moreover, at the BDC level, I want to see proven management teams that have strong track records, as well as low non-accruals, relatively low watch lists, and, perhaps most importantly, companies that are buying back their stock aggressively.

To me, this signals that these are the funds that have true confidence in their durability through the current down cycle and are working actively to unlock value for shareholders by putting money where their mouth is.

As a result, while BDCs and asset managers have been among some of my smaller sector allocations over the past few years, today I am tactfully allocating an increasing amount of capital to them while still maintaining prudent diversification in my portfolios that have delivered long-term total return outperformance along with below-market beta at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Market Finally Pricing In Recession - 2 Dividend Stocks To Weather The Storm
data.description:
Introduction

In the past week or two, the market has been seemingly on a downward spiral. In my opinion, I believe the market is finally starting to realize that the ongoing war with Iran could push us into a recession.

With oil & gas prices spiking, the dollar strengthening, and long-term treasury yields rising, a continuous war could be the tipping point. Previously, the market has shown astronomical resilience, going up even when reports haven't been that great.

Going forward, I think we will see further volatility. And if the war continues, it's likely to see rate cut plans halted and a potential recession in the near future as a result.

In this article, I discuss two safe-haven plays income-focused investors should consider and why.

Is The Market Finally Pricing In A Downturn?

As previously mentioned, the market has been quite resilient, going up on bad news and down on good news. For instance, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) dropped after reporting a blowout quarter with data center revenue growing 75% to $62.31 billion. And management expects this to increase by more than $10 billion in the next quarter.

In the past month, the S&P (SP500) has been down over 3%. After a double-digit year with gains above 16%, 2026 has not been so great as far as returns.

But as always, there are sectors and companies that perform well despite what the overall market is doing. Previously, I anticipated that we would see multiple rate cuts in 2026 due to the new Fed chair taking over in May and inflation moderating.

But now, not so much. If the war continues, it's likely we will see continued market volatility due to increasing inflation risks. And this could eventually push the economy into a recession.

JPMorgan's (JPM) CEO recently commented on this, and I must say I agree with him. The labor market saw further weakening with unemployment ticking up to 4.4%.

Previously, recession odds were below 20%. But according to CNBC, recession odds recently spiked due to rising oil prices on the prediction market platform, Kalshi. And if the war goes on, I expect odds to increase in the coming weeks.

As recession fears increase, we will likely see more volatility in the market. While I expect this to impact nearly all sectors, this is where dividend-paying stocks can help alleviate some of the pain.

This is because even as their share prices fall, quality dividend stocks are likely to continue paying, and even raising their dividends.

For context, this article is more about companies that will keep delivering income and can see potential price appreciation during uncertain times. So, this is not about those who are attractive bargains.

Without further ado, let's get into these two stocks.

#1 Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) - A Defensive Monthly Payer Yielding Nearly 4%

The first stock investors should consider is Agree Realty. While REITs have not had a memorable performance in recent years, many have performed well in recent months.

Particularly for ADC, the REIT has significantly outperformed the S&P in the past 6 months, up double-digits. While some of this could be because of prior enthusiasm regarding rate cuts, I believe part of this is due to their defensive portfolio.

ADC has 67% exposure to investment-rated tenants like Walmart Inc. (WMT), Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST), Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW), The Home Depot, Inc. (HD), and others. Their top 3 tenant sectors are grocery, home improvement, and convenience stores.

If we do end up in a recession, it's likely these tenants will continue to pay rent. Even during downturns, consumers still need essential items like food, home items, and necessities for their automobiles.

During their latest quarter, ADC reported occupancy of 99.7%. In a recession, this could increase, but I suspect ADC will continue business as usual. In Q4, their revenue grew 18.6% year-over-year, while AFFO grew 4.6%.

For 2026, management is currently guiding for higher AFFO growth of 5.3% with a $4.56 midpoint. They also expect acquisitions to be higher this year, between $1.4 billion and $1.6 billion.

Besides higher investment activity, ADC's best-in-class balance sheet ensures that it is likely to continue rewarding shareholders with dividend increases twice a year for the foreseeable future.

In 2025, ADC raised the dividend 3.6% and ended with a payout ratio of 71%. With a dividend yield close to 4%, investors are looking at potential total returns of around 9%–10% minimum.

Their A-rated balance sheet with no material debt for the next two years also puts them in a comfortable position if we see a recession. Again, investing in ADC is not about seeing significant price appreciation, but more so about preserving capital and getting a solid return.

#2 Waste Management, Inc. (WM) - A Defensive Trash Compounder

Waste Management, like Agree Realty, is also a defensive stock likely to do well in a recession. Not because of their portfolio, but the industry they operate in. No matter what happens, WM is likely to perform well as a collection & disposal operator.

As seen in the chart below, Waste Management, like ADC, is also up close to 12% in the past 6 months. Both companies' performances have been nearly identical, with WM slightly edging the REIT.

Back in January, the company reported its full-year earnings and delivered decent reports. Revenue grew modestly from $26.42 billion to $26.62 billion, while their bottom line increased close to 4% to $7.50.

But their cash flow performance was impressive, with cash from operations up double-digits from the prior year. This resulted in free cash flow increasing 26.8% from $2 billion to $2.95 billion. Their end-of-year payout ratio was 44.2%.

And going forward, management expects more acceleration with a 30% growth target for 2026. As a result, management raised the dividend close to 15%, impressive considering the challenging economic backdrop.

WM has a wide moat. In Q4, they continue to extend this by commissioning 7 new natural gas facilities to expand their renewable energy network. They also upgraded 5 recycling facilities and added new ones in 4 additional markets.

WM's balance sheet was in solid shape, with leverage increasing to 3.1x due to the Stericycle acquisition. While this is slightly above their targeted range of 2.75x - 3.0x, I expect them to reach this target by the end of 2026.

Liquidity was robust with $2.2 billion in revolver capacity and $22.9 billion in long-term debt. This is small in comparison to their market cap of nearly $100 billion. So, Waste Management is in a comfortable position should macro uncertainty increase in the coming months.

Price Action

As mentioned previously, both of these companies should be considered as investments due to their ability to preserve investors' capital. Should we see a steep crash, I do expect both stocks to see potential underperformance as well.

But due to their recession-resistant businesses, I expect both to fare better than the overall market and peers. Because of their quality, both trade at premiums and offer little upside.

For Agree Realty, the current upside is a little over 2%. But I expect shares to hover around the current level, likely in a range of $75–$80 a share.

For Waste Management, I foresee shares increasing if macro uncertainty increases. While significant upside is unlikely, I can see shares sitting between $245 - $255.

Investor Takeaway

Both Agree Realty and Waste Management should be considered due to their ability to deliver durable income in uncertain times. But both companies are also not immune to a recession.

If one comes to fruition, ADC could see occupancy rates decline if tenants are forced to file for bankruptcy. Although the likelihood is minimal due to their tenant credit quality.

Waste Management could see margin suppression should the economy continue to weaken. For Agree Realty, the company was forced to reduce its dividend during the Great Financial Crisis.

But ADC was a much different and smaller company back then. WM saw significant volatility due to lower volumes and revenue. However, like ADC, WM has evolved, led by acquisitions and natural energy expansion.

For investors looking for companies that can deliver durable income while potentially seeing less volatility compared to the overall market in a recession, consider these two dividend stocks for your portfolio.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls: The Trump Era And Strategic Need For Domestic Shipbuilding Capacity
data.description:
Intro

Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII) is America's largest military shipbuilder and the sole domestic manufacturer of nuclear-powered aircraft carriers. Their business has reached a critical inflection point as they transition from being viewed as a low-growth, legacy contractor to a critical piece of America's national security. The current administration has started an aggressive push towards a 381-ship fleet and has recently detailed America's Maritime Action Plan that fundamentally alters the economics of shipbuilding by heavily subsidizing shipyard infrastructure. On a more granular level, the Navy's cancellation of the failing Constellation-class program and its decision to pivot to using HII's design guarantees a new revenue stream for the business. HII's strong revenue visibility, combined with federally absorbed capex and growing margins from its Mission Technologies segment, has fundamentally altered HII into a high-leverage growth play that is of utmost importance to the future of our country.

Company Overview

HII is currently the largest military shipbuilding company in the United States. They operate through three primary segments: Newport News Shipbuilding (NNS), Ingalls Shipbuilding, and Mission Technologies. Each segment plays a distinct role in its defense ecosystem, creating a synergistic relationship where its legacy businesses fund their high-growth, high-margin expansion into autonomous systems and AI.

The Newport News Shipbuilding facility is the only manufacturing site in the Western Hemisphere capable of designing, building, and refueling nuclear-powered aircraft carriers. This is not just a factory but a nationally strategic asset that is a vital part of our defensive capabilities. Their flagship product is the Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier, which is the most expensive and technologically advanced warship ever created. These serve as mobile airbases, which allow America to assert power globally without relying on foreign ground bases. The barriers to entry for carrier construction are massive. NSS has a massive nuclear-certified workforce and countless supply chain relationships that are required to integrate the nuclear reactors with their aircraft launch systems. At the mid-point of a carrier's 50-year life, it must undergo a multi-billion-dollar process to refuel the reactors, modernize systems, and ensure safety. NNS is the only shipyard capable of fulfilling work that provides revenue sources beyond new shipbuilding orders. They are one of two shipyards capable of building nuclear-powered submarines. They operate under a teaming agreement with General Dynamics where NNS builds the stern, bow, sail, and habitable spaces while GD builds the reactor compartment. NNS also builds major components in the new Columbia-class ballistic missile submarine,s which are the Navy's "number one acquisition priority". This program is vital to national deterrence, especially regarding Chinese expansion in the Pacific. This protects the program from budget cuts and provides HII with a very safe revenue stream.

The Ingalls Shipbuilding segment is the "workhorse" for the U.S. Navy. This segment focuses on conventionally powered warships. They help manufacture the Arleigh Burke-Class Destroyers, which provide air defense, anti-submarine warfare, and many other strike capabilities. Ingalls is currently the sole builder of large-deck amphibious assault ships and the primary builder of amphibious transport docks. These both align with the U.S. Marine Corps' goals towards modernization of their weapons and enable the deployment of fighter jets from sea. HII competes primarily with General Dynamics for destroyers but remains the primary builder on four other ship classes.

HII has recognized that the future of warfare will be dominated by autonomous systems and AI and has established its Mission Technologies segment for this reason. They produce the Remus line of unmanned underwater vehicles. Recently, the Remus 300 was selected by the U.S. Navy's "Lionfish" program. Additionally, the Romulus line of unmanned surface vessels is well-positioned to meet the Navy's demand for low-cost warfare options. They also have many software options that capture additional value from providing the "brains" of the ship as well. These options span across command, control, computers, communications, cyber, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance in what they call their C5ISR solutions. By leveraging their manufacturing process with acquired autonomy software, they are creating a vertically integrated solution that pure software companies cannot match.

The Trump Era

The core of the investment thesis around HII revolves around a dramatic shift in U.S. fiscal policy. The Trump Administration has viewed shipbuilding capacity as a proxy for national power and has enacted a range of executive actions and legislative proposals that were designed to revive the sector. This policy is extremely beneficial for HII as they remain the strongest domestic provider for these services. On April 9th, 2025, President Trump signed the "Restoring America's Maritime Dominance" Executive Order. This directly created the "Maritime Prosperity Zones", which are designed to attract private capital into shipbuilding infrastructure by offering massive tax incentives. Investors can defer or reduce capital gains taxes if they invest in qualified maritime funds that deploy capital into these shipyards. This investment goes directly into new housing, upgrading dry docks, and expanding steel fabrication shops using these tax-advantaged dollars. This lowers HII's effective cost of capital and will fund the training for the next generation of nuclear engineers and welders. The executive order also establishes a "Maritime Security Trust Fund," which is financed by tariffs and fees levied on foreign-built vessels that are docking at U.S. ports. These fees have primarily been targeting China's predatory shipbuilding practices and give a steady stream of liquidity. This revived policy environment is not just about subsidies but also about a massive influx of orders. The Trump Administration has stated that they are assembling a "Golden Fleet" and plan to move beyond their previous target of 355 ships to a target of 381 manned ships and 134 unmanned vessels. The current fleet size is ~300 and provides a large opportunity for growth given HII's positioning as the premium shipbuilder in the United States.

The Frigate Opportunity

Recently, the U.S. Navy cancelled the Constellation-class frigate program, citing massive delays and design instability from HII's competitor, Fincantieri Marinette Marine. The ship kept growing in weight and complexity, with deliveries being pushed back to FY2029. The Navy deemed this unacceptable given the proximity of a potential conflict with China. Facing this clear gap in their arsenal, the Navy is now requiring an American design that can be in the water by 2028. There is only one production line in the United States that is capable of meeting these expectations on both domestic production and timeline. HII has built 10 NSCs for the Coast Guard. These designs are mature and have been in production for years. HII has proposed a militarized version of the NSC for over a decade, and this design would fill the requirements of the Navy. Because the hull form is proven, HII can leverage its existing designs to meet the 2028 deadline and remain a prime candidate for securing this contract. If we assume the same cost as the previous Constellation program, this could be a $22B opportunity in additional incremental revenue over the next decade. The primary competitor for these contracts is Austal USA, which has recently pivoted to steel shipbuilding and is suggesting a frigate based on its Independence-class ships or a new design. Austal's steel line is still in its infancy and has faced issues in the past. For a Navy that is prioritizing certainty given global tensions, HII's mature NSC line is a far safer option compared to Austal's somewhat unproven developmental capabilities.

Financial Analysis

HII's recent momentum has proved a decoupling from its historical low-growth baseline. In Q4 FY2025, HII reported revenue of $3.48B (up 15.7% YoY) alongside net income increasing to $159M (+29.3% YoY). The company's balance sheet carries a significant cash position of $774M compared to its total debt position of $2.7B. This leveraged profile is standard for a capital-intensive critical government contractor, as they have a steady stream of reliable government receivables, which ensure liquidity. For FY2026, management has guided for free cash flow in the range of $500M-$600M and total shipbuilding revenue of $9.7B-$9.9B. Management has warned of a negative free cash flow swing expected in Q1 FY2026 because customer payments were aggressively pulled forward into Q4 to pad year-end numbers. This is what led to the bad earnings report reaction, although it recovered the bulk of the move within a few days. Segment operating margins expanded to 5.6% in Q4 FY2025, compared to 3.4% in Q4 FY2024. More importantly, segment operating income surged 89.3% YoY to $195M. This inflection point is largely driven by working past low-margin legacy contracts that were signed before recent inflation, alongside catch-up adjustments on the Virginia-class submarine program. For the full year FY2025, HII generated $800M in FCF. This restored cash flow provides the liquidity to ramp up their high-margin Mission Technologies segment. Currently, HII also has a backlog of $55.7B, which heavily insulates their business from any issues and guarantees a decade of visibility as Washington continues to grow their naval capacity.

Model

In our model, we consider two scenarios: a base case that assumes a standard execution of their existing backlog without major new frigate revenue in FY2026 and an accelerated case that prices in immediate, aggressive contracting from the Navy in order to hit their FY2028 target.

Scenario A: The Base Case

Metric Q4 FY2025 Q1 FY2026 Q2 FY2026 Q3 FY2026 Q4 FY2026
Total Revenue ($B) 3.48 3.05 3.15 3.25 3.55
Shipbuilding Revenue ($B) 2.75 2.30 2.35 2.45 2.70
Mission Technology Revenue ($B) 0.73 0.75 0.80 0.80 0.85
Operating Margin 5.6% 5.5% 5.8% 6.1% 6.4%
Operating Income ($M) 195 168 183 198 227
Free Cash Flow ($M) 760 -550 250 350 500

The projected negative $550M FCF in Q1 reflects management's warning of customer payments being pulled forward in Q4 FY2025. We expect operating margins to expand steadily from 5.5% to 6.4% as their legacy contracts are replaced with new ones.

Scenario B: The Accelerated Case

Metric Q4 FY2025 Q1 FY2026 Q2 FY2026 Q3 FY2026 Q4 FY2026
Total Revenue ($B) 3.48 3.05 3.25 3.50 3.95
Shipbuilding Revenue ($B) 2.75 2.30 2.45 2.70 3.10
Mission Technology Revenue ($B) 0.731 0.75 0.80 0.80 0.85
Operating Margin 5.6% 5.5% 6.2% 6.7% 7.2%
Operating Income ($M) 195 168 201 234 284
Free Cash Flow ($M) 760 -550 300 450 650

In Q3 and Q4, shipbuilding revenues jumped significantly from the new frigate programs. The initial phases of emergency defense contracts such as this one often guarantee a profit margin on top of all expenses, which immediately pushed operating margins above 7% by year-end. This is a scenario where the Navy demands rapid procurement of these new ships and begins paying HII immediately to get them built as soon as possible.

Valuation

For a sector plagued with capital-intensive reinvestment and depreciation constantly distort bottom line earnings, we use P/S to cut through the noise. Assuming a current market capitalization of $17.3B, we derive two FWD P/S ratios given each of our scenarios. In our base case, HII is valued at a FWD P/S of 1.33x. In our accelerated case, HII is valued at a P/S of 1.25x. Both of these valuation metrics are historically rich for HII and the general shipbuilding industry. Limiting the company to these traditional ranges fundamentally mispriced the company's new reality. Under the Trump administration, HII demands a structural re-rating due to three main reasons. Huntington Ingalls holds an essential monopoly on U.S. naval power and is one of the only shipyards in the Western Hemisphere that is capable of meeting the demands of the U.S. government. Another aspect of this re-rating is concerned with the discount that they had due to relentless CapEx cycles. However, the Trump administration's executive orders and subsequent Maritime Security Trust Fund completely flip this dynamic. When the government absorbs the CapEx burden, HII's free cash flow yield should rise rapidly, therefore justifying a higher multiple. Lastly, as HII captures more of the Navy's shift towards autonomous warfare, it will secure higher-margin revenue. These factors combined lead us to estimate an eventual long-term re-rating towards a P/S of 1.75. In our base case, this leads us to a price target of ~$579.77 (+31.7% from current levels). In our accelerated case, this leads to a price target of $613.21 (+39.3% from current levels).

Risks

One of the biggest risks for HII is the bottleneck of skilled labor. Although the administration is offering massive tax incentives for infrastructure, this does not directly produce nuclear-certified welders, pipefitters, and structural engineers. If HII cannot staff their production lines, they will face the same scheduling delays and cost overruns that we have seen in the past. Another risk is a fall through of the broader naval budget in the case of political gridlock or shifting priorities. This would reduce their incremental revenue opportunity and could stunt further growth prospects. Other than these main points, it will be important to monitor margins and FCF yield going forward to ensure there is no compression due to outside factors.

Conclusion

Huntington Ingalls Industries currently stands at a massive inflection point in its business. The traditional narrative of HII as a slow-growth, capital-heavy legacy government contractor is being completely changed by aggressive federal intervention and the macro need for more ships. The administration's push for a 381-ship manned fleet and injection of capital through Maritime Prosperity Zones drastically lowers HII's infrastructure burden and transfers some capex risk to the federal government. Alongside this, the failure of the Constellation-class frigate acts as an immediate opportunity for optionality within the stock, as they can fill this near-term procurement gap that the Navy needs. Given a re-rating to a 1.75 P/S multiple, our accelerated model proposes a price target just north of $600. In our base case, we have a price target for HII at ~$580. The main hurdle remains labor execution as HII needs to secure specialized labor to meet its aggressive timelines. At these levels, the stock presents a highly asymmetrical risk-reward profile backed by an impenetrable domestic monopoly on naval shipbuilding capacity.

data.title: Lam Research: Supply-Constrained WFE Cycle Sets Up A Multi-Year Runway
data.description:
Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) has benefited strongly from the AI-driven capital expenditure cycle on memory. It has gained 194% in the past year, outperforming the broader semiconductor index, which grew 86%. This rally has drawn skepticism as investors have questioned the durability of the AI build-out. But, we are buyers of the recent dip as we think the company is sitting at the very intersection of nearly every structural growth vector in semi, like HBM stacking, NAND layer scaling, advanced package, and gate-all-around (GAA) transistor architectures.

Ten quarters of consecutive growth

Lam Research delivered an exceptional quarter in FY2Q26 (CY4Q25) with revenue up 22% Y/Y to $5.34B, ahead of market estimates of $5.23B. Non-GAAP gross margins were 49.7%, which expanded 220bps Y/Y. Non-GAAP operating margin reached 34.3%, compared to 30.7% a year ago. Similarly, EPS grew to $1.27, beating estimates by 9%.

…and then some more.

The March 2026 quarter was guided to $5.7B in revenue at the midpoint, which was well above the consensus of $5.2B at the time of the earnings call. Non-GAAP gross margin was expected to be ~49%, and EPS was guided to reach $1.35. The slight dip in gross margins was explained as a shift in customer mix and not pricing pressure. The management emphasized product and customer mix as more important than sheer volume on a Q/Q basis, and the business is not truly about a fixed-cost leverage story.

The expanding WFE cycle is constrained by supply

CEO Tim Archer commented positively on the wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) outlook. The initial view on CY2026 WFE is up ~23% Y/Y to $135B from $110B in CY2025. It is implied that demand exceeds this view as the industry is currently supply-constrained on clean room availability. Major customers like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) and Intel Corporation (INTC) are likely behind schedule on the physical construction of new fabrication facilities, which indicates that tool shipments are delayed.

We note that the industry is effectively sold out and customers are entering multi-year agreements to secure equipment capacity, with clean room constraint easing up in 2027, as the CEO has stated.

The company is also second-half weighted (fiscal year), meaning CY1Q26 – CY2Q26 would potentially exceed the current exit rate imputed from CY4Q25.

Three vectors enlarging its SAM

The company has confidently pointed out that its served available market (SAM) is growing rapidly. We believe they have three growth vectors that stand out:

Gate-all-around (GAA) transistors: Front-end manufacturing has undergone a significant inflection through the transition from FinFET to GAA transistor architectures. The management has quantified this opportunity at about $1B of incremental SAM per 100,000 wafer starts per month (WSPM).

As customers have been accelerating GAA migration timelines, Lam has shipped more than $1B in GAA nodes and advanced packaging in CY2024, before tripling in CY2025. This trend is anticipated to continue in CY2026, with Lam's core competencies outshining as GAA requires more etch and deposition steps.

Advanced packaging: Being one of the fastest-growing segments in the WFE market, the company believes its AP revenue will grow more than 40% in CY2026, helped by HBM stacking, which is dominated by Lam's leading advantages in electroplating and through-silicon via etch; chiplets interconnection, and hybrid bonding. As advanced packaging increases from a mid-single-digit share of foundry spending, Lam is poised to benefit meaningfully, given its dominance.

HBM4 and memory scaling: The technology is moving to 16-layer HBM4/4E, which introduces incremental TSV etch, deposition, and electroplating steps for each layer. Fortunately, these are the specialties of the company. Separately, the AI inference cycle is sparking NAND bit demand, and the management puts a rough estimate of 1 percentage point of additional bit growth for every 2-3 million AI accelerators deployed. Non-volatile contextual memory, which can extend to terabyte-scale caches, will be used for inferencing. As NAND upgrades jumped more than 90% Y/Y in CY2025, the management sees an opportunity in a $40B install base upgrading.

To conclude, Lam's share in the WFE market likely expanded 1%+ from low-30s to mid-30s, with high-30s as a multi-year target.

CSBG: The counter-cyclical earnings anchor

While it is natural for investors to be wary of semi-spend cycles, the Customer Support Business Group (CSBG) provides an attractive floor for the company's earnings model. The segment generates revenue of ~$2B per quarter or $7.2B in a record year in CY2025. This business serves Lam's ever-increasing global base by offering spares, upgrades, and maintenance services.

Even if there is a disruption in customer spending, likely from budgetary constraints, overheated markets, or clean room delays, CSBG benefits when the customer decides to upgrade/maintain their existing equipment instead.

This business is margin accretive, and there are ongoing improvements like increased automation and intelligent platforms to further improve operating leverage.

Risks that we are watching out for

Macro environment: Lam operates in an industry that is supported by dense backlogs, but investors have grown cautious on the overall AI capex splurge, and if the end market cannot deliver the returns, upstream companies will begin to feel the downsizing. There are other external factors outside of the control of the company/industry, including geopolitical tensions.

Capex changes: The company has guided 4%-5% of revenue as capex (~$260M-$290M per quarter) for manufacturing expansion, which is in line with the prior year trend. We believe that this is reasonable, but further upward adjustments here (e.g., 5%-6% or more) would need to be justified as the market grows cautious on late-cycle semi capex. FCF is expected to grow more than 20% a year, while its TTM FCF yield is 2.3%, slightly ahead of NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) 2.1%.

China is 35% of revenue in the December quarter: The equipment for China tends to be mature (less growth) and is thus unlikely to be the subject of export controls. We think the company is managing this geographical segment well, and as time goes on, the portion of revenue from China will naturally become smaller.

Competitive positioning: Lam still maintains a healthy lead due to its expertise in etch and deposition via its Akara platform, which is one of the most advanced conductor etch tools available in the market, and via its ALTUS Halo platform, which is the world's first molybdenum ALD tool in production, allowing barrier-free molybdenum at scale and addressing historical deposition challenges. Lam is arguably one of the most favorable among its equipment peers due to GAA, HBM, and advanced packaging. Other competitors are also scoring wins, so it is likely that the entire pie is still growing.

Memory constraints may dampen equipment demand: Paradoxically, memory shortages can delay NAND equipment orders if customers prefer conversion/upgrades over installing new capacity. Although this is partially offset by its CSBG.

Valuation and verdict

LRCX is trading at peak cycle valuations of ~31X-40X forward P/E, compared to its five-year mean of 22X. In the previous cycles, the company often experiences 40% growth in peak years, and revenue was more volatile, whereas the revenue growth in this cycle is more durable and predictable.

Revenue growth rate is in the 20s for the next few years, which indicates a prolonged bull market for WFE. We expect sales to grow north of 20% in FY26E/FY27E, similar to what the consensus says, because we expect the total WFE market to increase to $135B for 23% Y/Y and Lam to at least maintain (or slightly increase) its share. The trend will likely persist in FY27E due to secular drivers behind GAA transition and memory scaling, but there will be a swing factor depending on the exact delivery of the clean room ramp in H2 CY2026 and the success of NAND recovery/HBM scaling.

We opine that the company has one of the most favorable technologies among its peers, so its growth profile is the most attractive and robust. Additionally, the company returns ~85% of its free cash flow to shareholders via dividends and share buybacks, providing a meaningful valuation buffer.

We stay with the fundamentally improving trends and rate LRCX a BUY.

Metric FY2026E FY2027E FY2028E
Revenue $22.4B $27.5B $31.0B
EPS $5.31 $6.90 $8.11
P/E 40x 31x 27x

Source: Himalayas Research estimates

data.title: Talen Energy: All Roads Lead To Higher Energy Prices
data.description:
Introduction

The IPPs (independent power producers) Talen Energy (TLN), Vistra (VST), and Constellation Energy (CEG), all of which I have covered on several occasions, have been treading water since mid-2025, despite the AI infrastructure theme being a clear indicator of higher electricity prices through 2030. The main pushback I have seen and flagged as a risk factor is political, with energy price increases, leading to a call to somehow make the data centers pay for this energy or, better yet, build their own. For many investors, this may mean the IPPs will not see the expected pricing gains. I believe this is a fundamental error; IPPs, by definition, have the flexibility to sell their energy to the highest bidder into wholesale grids or directly to end users under PPAs (power purchase agreements), as is the case with Talen’s AWS deal.

Structural Energy Deficit

As long as the market remains structurally tight, with demand exceeding supply, prices will rise. The near-term solutions for some existing/new data centers are to build their own energy sources, such as generators, which is where Caterpillar (CAT) is present, and FTAI (FTAI) has initiated, or to use batteries to store energy during off-peak hours, but these have limited scope and higher cost. Bloom Energy's (BE) technology may also act as a stopgap, but it's not fully proven. Building SMRs (small modular reactors) or even combined-cycle gas power plants is at least five years away. GE Vernova (GEV) and Siemens Energy (SMEGF) are booked solid, while wind and solar have limitations and are unwelcome by the current government.

Requiring data centers to pay for the higher cost is a good way to reduce consumer pain, but it would not impact IPPs or even regulated utilities from benefiting from those higher prices; if anything, this regulation could accelerate the PPA process. The main sticking point is that at some moment there will be an energy deficit, and I would assign a low probability that consumers will be blacked out over the data center. A high degree of backup or redundancy will be needed.

The alternative is to limit data center build-out, which would greatly reduce that whole ecosystem's growth, penetration, and promises of AI productivity heaven.

Operating Model Update

It seems that every few months, Talen buys generation assets at good valuations with cheap leverage or with their more expensive shares as currency, and I need to update the estimates, which the consensus largely ignores until Talen officially closes the transaction. In my last update, I added the Freedom/Guernsey acquisition, which is now incorporated in the 2026 consensus. However, TLN acquired 2.6 GW of gas-fired plants in January, which they expect to close mid-year. Thus, I added 50% of this new capacity at the current forecasted revenue per GW. Note that this is a simplified model; the company sells capacity and generation at varying prices and costs. What makes the estimates easier is that 2026 revenue and costs (natural gas) have been 90% hedged, locked in. There should not be negative surprises unless they can't deliver the energy.

In addition to adding the Cornerstone acquisition, I also assume the average price per GW will increase 8% vs the consensus 3% in 2027-2028. Which may be conservative if the AI data center build-out meets half its target, which calls for a 10%-12% increase in total load needs. The combination adds $400m to EBITDA each year.

The incorporation of this 2.6 GW, which is 19% of current capacity, will have a full impact in 2027 and beyond. The company stated that this acquisition would be accretive to cash flow in 2026, adding 15% to cash flow per share.

For 2027, there is a greater risk and opportunity with 40% of revenue/costs hedged. If the price rises, they can book higher cash flow and earnings. It's been my experience that Talen hedges on a rolling 12-month basis, so by mid-2026 50% of next year's revenue will be hedged. This more aggressive commercial strategy is why I have a larger investment in TLN than in VST.

Valuation

The consensus has a $463 YE26 price target that backs into a 1x PEG (excluding this year's extraordinary gains due to M&A). Using my estimates as discussed earlier, and that takes into account the $526m in SBC share dilution granted at the end of 2025, I arrive at a $466 price target. I'm a bit more conservative on the PEG and P/CE (cash earnings: net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation). Nonetheless, as seen in the peer comps section, TLN is one of the fastest-growing electric utilities and may deserve a premium valuation given its excellent operating and acquisition execution and flexibility to sell its energy to the best offer.

Peer Comps

The table below utilizes consensus data for all the stocks. As can be seen, the IPPs (CEG, VST, and TLN) have the highest growth rates and seem to be the main winners of the AI data center buildout. The regulated utilities do not have the flexibility to sell energy to the highest bidder and are required, in most cases, to meet their concession-area demand (i.e., capex) at a regulated return. In terms of valuation, except for CEG, most are in a similar absolute multiple (P/CE or EV/EBITDA), but many utilize relative or PEG ratios above TLN and VST.

Risk

I view regulation as the main risk to TLN and IPPs in general. If a severe energy deficit occurs, regulators/governments may force them to hand over their energy rather than remaining independent. The next greatest risk is if the energy constraints lead to a downshift in data center build-out, at least in the US. A closer balance of supply and demand may keep prices lower and hurt TLN's cash flow growth. Natural gas cost may be a concern, but it can usually be passed on to prices.

Conclusion

I rate Talen a buy. As stated in several other articles, I believe TLN is best leveraged to rising electricity prices in the US and is executing an aggressive commercial strategy and asset acquisitions that may lead to over 20% annual cash flow growth, which is not priced into the share price.

data.title: Defense Lifts Moat Stocks As Tech Stumbles
data.description:
Valuation discipline and sector allocation tilted exposure towards consumer staples, industrials and health care, supporting gains as software lagged.

* Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance.

U.S. equity markets experienced a notable divergence in February. An accelerating rotation out of mega-cap technology stocks and into more defensive and cyclical areas of the market drove starkly different outcomes across benchmarks. The S&P 500 declined 0.76% during the month, pulled lower by its heavy concentration in technology names, while the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index rose 3.55%, underscoring the breadth of participation outside of the largest constituents. The NASDAQ Composite fell more than 3%, reflecting the pronounced weakness among technology and software companies.

Concerns around artificial intelligence disruption of traditional software business models intensified early in the month, triggering a multi-day selloff in enterprise software names that extended a pattern of weakness that had been building in recent months. Leadership came from sectors positioned away from the AI disruption narrative, with utilities, energy, materials, and consumer staples all gaining between 8% and 10%.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) gained 2.13% in February, outperforming the S&P 500 by nearly 3 percentage points as the Index's equal-weighted, valuation-conscious approach proved well suited to the month's rotation away from mega-cap technology. Sector allocation was the overwhelming driver of relative performance, as the strategy's substantial overweights in consumer staples and industrials contributed meaningfully. The Moat Index's overweight to health care also provided a tailwind, while its underweight to information technology, which declined roughly 3.6%, was beneficial on a relative basis. Year-to-date through February, the Moat Index has gained 3.38%, leading the S&P 500's 0.68% return.

The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the "SMID Moat Index") rose 1.11% in February but trailed both the S&P MidCap 400, which gained 4.12%, and the S&P SmallCap 600, which rose 2.17%. Small- and mid-cap stocks broadly outperformed large-caps during the month, consistent with the ongoing rotation into more cyclical and value-oriented areas of the market. Within the SMID Moat Index, stock selection was the primary headwind to relative performance this month, with weakness concentrated among information technology holdings. Sector allocation was modestly positive, with the strategy's overweight to materials and underweight to financials contributing favorably.

Defensive Leadership Lifts Moat Strategies in February

Source: Morningstar. Data as of 2/28/2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Please see index definitions and other important disclosures at the end of this content. Fund performance current to the most recent month end is available by visiting vaneck.com or by calling 800.826.2333.

Moat Index Highlights: Earnings Strength Overcomes Software Drag

In February, relative performance within the Moat Index was driven almost entirely by sector allocation, while stock selection was effectively neutral. Overweights in consumer staples and industrials were the primary contributors to relative performance versus the S&P 500, while the strategy's overweight to health care and underweight to information technology also proved beneficial during a month in which the cap-weighted benchmark was weighed down by its heavy concentration in technology names.

Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT) was the top contributor to Moat Index performance during the month, with shares rising approximately 16%. The company reported strong quarterly earnings results in mid-February and provided an impressive outlook for 2026, with management guiding for more than 20% growth in equipment sales driven by an accelerating AI infrastructure buildout cycle. Investor enthusiasm reflected growing confidence in a sustained, multi-year expansion in wafer fabrication equipment demand, as AI chip supply constraints continue to far outstrip available capacity. Morningstar views Applied Materials' position as the world's largest and most diversified supplier of wafer fabrication equipment as central to its wide economic moat, underpinned by intangible assets from its industry-leading research and development spending and steep switching costs from the complexity of its equipment and embedded customer relationships.

Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. (BMY) was the second-largest contributor, with shares gaining approximately 13%. The company reported full-year 2025 results in early February that demonstrated its ability to hold revenue roughly steady despite significant headwinds from generic competition for legacy oncology drugs. Growth in newer therapies, including Camzyos in cardiology, Reblozyl in hematology, and Breyanzi and Opdualag in oncology, reinforced the company's ability to diversify beyond maturing franchises. An active late-stage pipeline with numerous catalysts expected through the end of 2026 also supported investor sentiment, as the market increasingly looks toward Bristol-Myers' trajectory beyond the patent cliffs for Eliquis and Opdivo in 2028. Morningstar assigns Bristol-Myers a wide economic moat, supported by a broad lineup of patent-protected drugs, an entrenched salesforce, and economies of scale, and views shares as undervalued heading into a year filled with pipeline readouts.

Other notable contributors during the month included Clorox Co. (CLX), a household cleaning and consumer products company; United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS), a global package delivery and logistics provider; and The Hershey Co. (HSY)), a confectionery and snack food company whose shares surged more than 22% following encouraging quarterly results and improving sentiment around easing cocoa cost headwinds.

Companies detracting the most from Moat Index performance in February were concentrated within technology and software, reflecting the broader market's intensifying concerns around AI disruption of traditional enterprise software business models. Workday Inc. (WDAY), a human capital management and financial software firm, was the largest detractor, with shares falling roughly 24%. Adobe Inc. (ADBE)), a digital media and creative software company; Salesforce Inc. (CRM), a provider of enterprise cloud software; and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) also weighed on results, as each was caught in the rolling selloff that impacted software names throughout the month. LPL Financial Holdings Inc. (LPLA), a brokerage platform supporting independent financial advisors, was the only non-software detractor among the bottom five.

SMID Moat Index Highlights: Defensive Contributors Offset Tech Weakness

The SMID Moat Index posted a positive return in February, supported by contributions from consumer staples, health care and industrial holdings, though performance trailed small- and mid-cap benchmarks as stock selection within information technology weighed heavily. Sector allocation was modestly positive during the month, while the overall shortfall relative to benchmarks was attributable to company-specific weakness among several technology-oriented names.

The Hershey Co. (HSY) was the top contributor to SMID Moat Index performance, with shares rising more than 22%. Fourth-quarter results included 6% organic sales growth, and management's fiscal 2026 outlook called for more than 30% growth in adjusted earnings per share, signaling that the worst of the cocoa inflation headwinds may be easing. Morningstar views Hershey's dominant position in the U.S. confectionery aisle, where it holds more than one-third of chocolate market share against minimal private-label competition, as the foundation of its wide economic moat, underpinned by strong intangible brand assets and an entrenched retail distribution network.

Generac Holdings Inc. (GNRC) was the second-largest contributor, with shares advancing approximately 34% during the month. The company reported fourth-quarter earnings that highlighted growing traction in the data center backup power market, with management guiding for 30% growth in commercial and industrial sales in 2026 as it executes against its growing backlog. Morningstar assigns Generac a narrow moat, supported by its dominant brand in home standby generators and cost advantages stemming from its unmatched scale in sales and distribution within the category.

Other notable contributors included Hasbro Inc. (HAS), a toy and entertainment company benefiting from its shift toward higher-margin digital gaming properties; Royalty Pharma PLC (RPRX), a buyer of biopharmaceutical royalties; and Zimmer Biomet Holdings Inc. (ZBH), a medical device company specializing in orthopedic implants.

Detractors from SMID Moat Index performance during February included several technology and software names, consistent with the pattern observed in the Moat Index. EPAM Systems Inc. (EPAM), a provider of digital platform engineering and software development services, was the largest detractor, with shares falling more than 32%. Zoom Communications Inc. (ZM), a provider of video communications and collaboration tools, declined roughly 20%, while Mattel Inc. (MAT), a toy manufacturer, fell approximately 19%. Workday Inc. (WDAY) and LPL Financial Holdings Inc. (LPLA) also detracted. The weakness among technology holdings reflected the same AI disruption concerns that pressured software names across the broader market, as investors reassessed the viability of traditional software business models in the face of rapidly advancing AI capabilities.

data.title: Eurozone Households Are First To Feel The Pain Of The Current Oil Price Shock
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro and Franziska Biehl, Senior Economist, Germany

Fuel prices are surging again, and for many households, filling up now hurts almost as much as during the 2022 energy crisis

It’s a fast-moving environment, which currently only allows to think in terms of scenarios rather than conviction calls. And while forecasters around the globe are currently grappling to adjust their macro forecasts – uncertain about how long the war in the Middle East and the disruption to the Strait of Hormuz will last – one part of the economy is already feeling the economic pain: households. More precisely, car-driving households. Any surge in oil prices is felt most immediately in one place – the petrol station.

Pain at the pump for European consumers

Our colleague Warren Patterson has written extensively on recent oil price developments and the possible scenarios ahead. We also know that it takes a while for the full macro impact of higher energy prices to unfold. Industrial companies typically hedge (at least some of) their energy purchases in forward markets, while households’ utility contracts usually run for one or two years. This was, for example, one of the reasons why during the 2022 energy crisis, it was energy companies that ran into financial trouble first, as they were unable to pass on higher procurement costs to end consumers. One place where consumers are not shielded by hedging strategies or longer-term contracts, however, is at the petrol station.

Even before oil prices reached this week’s painful highs, the jump in retail fuel prices had already pushed the cost of a standard 50-litre tank of petrol to levels last seen in 2022 in several major eurozone economies. Compared with the week before the joint US-Israeli strike on Iran, the price of a tank of unleaded petrol has risen by between €4.50 in Italy and €13.00 in Germany. For diesel, the increase has been even steeper, ranging from €8.40 in Italy to €21.50 in Germany. Relative to the 2025 annual average, German households have been hit particularly hard: they are now paying roughly €17.00 more per tank of unleaded petrol and around €28.00 more for diesel than they did on average last year. Dutch households are facing a similarly sharp increase in fuel costs.

The fact that prices within the eurozone, and across fuel types, vary widely in general is also due to excise duties, for which there is a minimum threshold of €0.359 per litre of unleaded petrol and of €0.330 per litre of diesel set by the EU. However, most member states have set higher values, resulting in an average excise duty on petrol of €0.570 per litre and of €0.468 per litre on diesel. At €0.845 per litre, the duty on unleaded petrol is highest in the Netherlands, while at €0.673, Italy has the highest duty on diesel. These tax differentials are the main reason why two households facing the same global oil price can still experience very different fill-up costs.

The extent to which these price increases cut into household budgets also depends heavily on how much people drive per year. At almost 14,000 kilometres, Austria tops the 2023 list – the most recent date for which comparable data is available, followed by Germany with around 12,500 kilometres per year. This means the same price increase hits Austrian and German households much harder than, for example, Italians, who drive only around 8,000 km per year.

Looking at driving behaviour in recent years shows that cutting fuel costs by simply driving less is unlikely. Significant deviations from the long-term average only occurred during the pandemic years, when widespread working-from-home arrangements reduced the need to commute. Gasoline prices were not a motive. Since then, mileage patterns have started to normalise, suggesting limited scope for households to offset higher prices by changing their driving habits.

Ultimately, this implies that eurozone households will once again need to devote a larger share of their disposable income to fuel, with German households facing the largest increase. For them, the share of disposable income spent at the fuel pump is expected to rise to 3.5%, up from 2.8% last year. Overall, the share of disposable income devoted to fuel ranged from around 2% in the Netherlands and 4.5% in Portugal last year.

Drivers might slam the brake on any recovery in private consumption

Although we currently assume in our base-case scenario that the impact of the war in the Middle East on markets, and thus on the eurozone economy, should not be long-lasting, the recent rise in energy prices is likely to put additional strain on consumer confidence, which is already markedly low. Given the old adage that gasoline prices rise like a rocket but fall like a feather, it is not just confidence that is being hit, but actual purchasing power.

Content Disclaimer
