data.title: PepsiCo: Improving, But Core Problems Remain
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), I rated them a Strong Sell, highlighting their unsustainable levels of dividend growth and expensive debt that keeps piling up, as even though macro improvements and recent activist investor involvement have potential to help, their recovery remains uncertain.

With visible improvements, cost cuts, and a solid restaging plan for their iconic brands, PEP stands to benefit from a potential recovery in demand, especially if backed by broader macro improvements.

However, I believe the company is still a Strong Sell, as even though I see solid improvements, they still aren’t enough to justify buying it yet, with little room for error despite significant long-term headwinds that can fundamentally change this industry, while the company will always have to pay back most of what it makes back to its shareholders if they want to keep their Dividend King status.

Internal Developments

PEP reported a solid Q4 overall, beating the market’s estimates and delivering $7.67 billion in free cash flow compared to $7.19 billion in 2024, or about $9.32 billion last year when adjusting for working capital changes, which is not bad in this environment.

Meanwhile, the company is taking several steps to adapt to the ongoing macro pressure, including the restaging of several iconic brands, offering better-for-you alternatives (in various ways, from using different oils to lower sugar options, higher protein, etc.) and even reducing the price of some snacks and beverages, aiming at improving their affordability given the current demand headwinds, as mentioned during their Q4 Earnings Call:

This is part of a multi-vector strategy to drive category growth and then obviously, our participation in the category. And this is something we've been working on since Q2 or so of last year, testing at scale in some of our key markets. We think that for some consumers, low- and middle-income consumers, the biggest friction they have today in our category for faster penetration is affordability. So we have been testing multiple ways to give them affordability. (...) You should be thinking this on top of space gains, big space gains that we're getting through the partnership with our customers because of these investments in price. We're also investing, as we said in our prepared remarks, a lot in innovation, especially to provide more functionality, simpler ingredients, restage some of our larger brands.

Regarding the guidance, PEP expects significant shelf space improvements this year (double-digit for Frito-Lay thanks to its ongoing relaunch), with a 2% to 4% increase in organic net revenue, a 4% to 6% increase in organic constant currency EPS, and CAPEX below 5% of net revenue, with a FCF conversion ratio of at least 80%. Note that they expect this year to include a final, one-time tax payment of ~$1 billion related to the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act.

For the medium-term, PEP also aims at reaching a FCF conversion rate of over 90% by 2027, advancing on its cost savings program, tightening its working capital management, and improving its margins by at least 100 bps over the next 3 fiscal years. Given Elliott Management’s “intervention,” the company also expects to cut about 20% of its North American food SKUs, and I believe Elliott's entering this can definitely change the narrative.

Financially, based on PEP’s latest report, we continue to see an okay position, with current assets not covering their current liabilities due to the short-term debt obligations, with the long-term debt continuing to rise.

As mentioned before, this is one of their core problems in recent years, as something that started as a way to secure some liquidity at low rates during the pandemic transformed into higher and higher amounts of more and more expensive debt, with the total debt reaching ~$53 billion in 2025 compared to the pre-pandemic ~$33 billion.

The Dividend King Burden

Pepsi is a Dividend King, boasting 54 years of consecutive increases and a 7% CAGR in dividend per share from 2010 to 2026, although the yield stands at about 3.6% today, expecting to spend about $7.9 billion on dividends in 2026 and another $1 billion on buybacks, for a combined yield of about 4.08%.

Compared to their $7.67 billion free cash flow seen in 2025, none of that is sustainable, although the company continues to expect an improving FCF conversion ratio that should cover it, as I’ll show in the Valuation segment.

However, to make things worse, they announced a new and ramped-up buyback program, aiming at repurchasing $10 billion through February 2030.

Sure, they are focusing on higher-potential brands, expanding Poppi globally (prebiotic beverage with strong potential), there’s the stake in Celsius Holdings, Inc. (CELH) that I like, but even then, the company will always have to keep growing its cash flow very well just to be able to grow the dividend by a bit, while weak environments like the current one can close the gap between FCF and returns significantly, as the company ultimately depends on the economy given the discretionary nature of their products.

As mentioned before, the company’s borrowing costs are also affected by this environment, and we can already see them issuing debt with interest rates as high as 5.00%, which is massive for a company like PEP.

Although they don’t face some significant debt wall and the debt is spread well into the future (~$4 billion per year in notes for the foreseeable future), the longer the current high-rate environment persists for, the worse their interest expenses will be as they refinance, especially since they keep issuing a lot of additional debt every year - adding about $5.1 billion in long-term debt in 2025 compared to 2024, meaning a ~13.70% increase.

There’s certainly the possibility for them to deliver, but a bad environment can continue to make it harder, while the most likely scenario is likely “mediocrity” - a small increase every year with most of their FCF growth far from their historical ~7% levels. And all of that for a combined yield that barely beats treasuries nowadays, when the market presents so many opportunities.

Macro Developments

To make matters worse, although there have been some improvements in the broader macro environment and borrowing costs went down a bit, the current landscape is far from great for Pepsi and a majority of consumer-related businesses - regardless of industry -, and it’s no surprise that virtually everyone has been reporting issues especially in North America for years at this point, including Pepsi.

This is the mix of the normal effects of a high-rate environment combined with extra layers of uncertainty coming from the worries around Iran, Japan’s potential US treasury sell-off, tariffs, the weakening dollar, recession worries, and more, all of which have to be accounted for (i.e. through higher borrowing costs/treasury yields to adjust the demanded reward for the new risks).

Regarding Iran, I’ll also add that this has significant potential to lead to an even worse environment for Pepsi and its consumers, with shipping giants reportedly avoiding even the Suez Canal and instead taking the Cape of Good Hope route. Combined with the conflict itself and its effects on oil and gas prices (among others), we can see this spread into a global slowdown, marked by inflation, higher shipping costs, supply chain disruptions, or even worse.

As we can see based on recent data, even before this conflict, the US consumer confidence plummeted to the lowest levels since 2014, not long ago, with consumers being marred by significant levels of stress, rising costs of living, and more, culminating in significant trade-downs and pressure, especially for the lower-income families, further deepening the K-shaped economic discrepancy.

Meanwhile, GLP-1 medicine has been shown to have significant negative effects, especially for calorie-dense products, which can be an issue for a big chunk of Pepsi’s brands, as the biggest drops in grocery spending for households that took the medicine were seen in products like cookies and snacks (about 6.7% to 11.1%), while Cornell and Numerator noted:

Interestingly, we do not observe statistically significant increases in spending on traditionally healthier products, such as fresh produce or whole grains, across all adopters (...) However, among weight-loss users specifically, there is a modest increase in spending on fresh produce and yogurt. The primary dietary change appears to be a reduction in high-calorie items, which, by construction, shifts the proportional spending toward healthier options, even without a substantial increase in the quantity of those items. Essentially, the composition of their grocery baskets becomes healthier more by cutting back on unhealthy items than by adding more healthy ones.

Note that I really do not believe this can be that big by itself due to affordability and need (even with an impossible 100% adoption, the drop in volume wouldn’t be catastrophic), but combined with MAHA initiatives and a generally more health-conscious consumer, we can definitely see significant issues for Pepsi, which they are trying to adapt to through Poppi, Gatorade’s relaunch, SunChips, Quaker, and similar initiatives.

Not only that, but another major issue for legacy brands comes from retailers like Walmart Inc. (WMT) or The Kroger Co. (KR), which have been actively developing their own brands. Not only does it allow them to have better leverage when negotiating with legacy brand producers like Pepsi, thanks to artificially limiting their shelf space, but it also allows them to attract consumers through better value at first, gather significant amounts of data, and fill the gaps that may appear in the future through various other price points.

Private labels are probably the biggest threat to the industry right now, especially since these retailers don’t have to worry about the same kind of marketing costs, shelf/promo contracts, or competition as a classic producer.

On the bright side, Pepsi has been adapting to this environment and lowered costs not long ago to address the affordability issues after so many increases since the pandemic, and the tariff situation became clearer following the Supreme Court’s decision, despite the subsequent increase.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company is indeed adapting, and I compliment them for clearly being aware of these trends and not just sitting and watching their brands fall, there’s only so much they can do against fundamental shifts or widespread macro pressure. Similar to how Molson Coors Beverage Company's (TAP) CEO said recently regarding the effects of the previous tariffs on aluminum prices:

(...) When Midwest premium goes up 300%, there are no immediate actions you can take to action that, right?

Meanwhile, the Dividend King “burden” (having to increase regardless of what if they want to keep the status) can force them into bad acquisitions, and a few mistakes, more macro pressure or bad capital allocation can rapidly close the gap between returns and cash flows, and if they get to a point where the dividend compounds faster, then they’ll need a major move (e.g. selling/spinning off).

Valuation

For PEP’s DCF to Equity, based on their revenue, EPS and FCF conversion guidance, we should see about $9.44 billion in 2026, followed by another jump to about $11.25 billion in 2027 given the medium-term guidance, followed by a 4.5% CAGR until 2030 (similar to the market’s expectations) and a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 9% discount rate to consider the risks, balance sheet with mounting debt, and potential long-term macro headwinds, with 1.37 billion shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Note that I won’t account for any changes in net borrowings, as I want to highlight their “pure” cash flow generation potential and assume that they won’t have to rely on issuing debt going forward.

This way, we get to an equity value of $181.63 billion, for an intrinsic value of $132.58 per share, below the current $159.43 at the moment of writing this, leaving little-to-no room for error if we want to see an attractive yield from this, while a relatively generous 8% discount rate (despite the potential fundamental macro changes) gets us to $157.39, which is pretty much around today’s price.

Conclusions

PEP remains a Strong Sell, as even though Elliott’s visible involvement has potential to change their story, the risk-reward still doesn’t justify buying, with little to no margin of safety.

Is the dividend/buyback program sustainable? Kind of, depending on what growth rate they want to keep it at. Is it attractive? That’s another story, and it depends on the risk-reward you are happy with given the potential for macro shocks or fundamental changes in the industry, but the answer for me is no, given the environment we’re referring to and the better opportunities we can find.

data.title: TEI: Long-Term Potential, But May Struggle In The Short-Term
data.description:
The Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (TEI) is a closed-end fund that offers an interesting proposition for income-seeking investors. In short, the fund invests its assets in both U.S. dollar-denominated and local currency emerging market bonds and uses the profits that it earns from this investment strategy to provide its own shareholders with a very attractive 8.93% distribution yield. As most market watchers will immediately recognize, this yield is significantly higher than that of any of the major bond index funds, but it is unfortunately a bit lower than what many closed-end bond funds are able to boast. However, the fact that the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund includes emerging market bonds that are denominated in the currency of the emerging markets makes it somewhat unique among bond funds, and this aspect of the fund’s strategy could ultimately provide investors with both diversification benefits and potentially higher returns. As such, it might be worth considering for a portfolio today, despite the fact that there are other bond funds on the market with higher yields.

Understanding the Strategy of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund

The website for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund offers the following description of its strategy:

The fund seeks high, current income, with a secondary goal of capital appreciation, by investing under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its net assets in income-producing securities of sovereign or sovereign-related entities and private sector companies in emerging market countries.

This description clearly states that the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund primarily invests in a portfolio of “income-producing securities” issued by entities that are located in emerging market nations. While technically securities such as dividend-paying common equities provide their investors with a certain level of income, this phrasing almost always refers specifically to fixed-income securities, such as bonds and preferred stock. However, the description also states that the fund invests in sovereign securities, which specifically means securities issued by a government. Governments only issue debt securities, not equities. After all, an equity security confers ownership of the entity, and governments are never owned by individuals (especially not individuals who are citizens of another country). Thus, we can expect that TEI’s portfolio will consist primarily of government bonds issued by countries that are considered to be emerging markets.

The most recent annual report for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund makes it clear that the fund does pretty much just invest in bonds issued by the governments of emerging market nations. This document states that the fund had the following asset allocation on December 31, 2025:

4.4%

All of these countries are generally considered to be “emerging markets,” although admittedly, the definition of that term can be somewhat vague. The Corporate Finance Institute offers the following definition:

Countries that are transitioning from the “developing” phase to the “developed” phase.

This definition admittedly is not particularly helpful, especially because it is not exactly clear what makes a country “developed.” WhyEduFinance states that there is no agreed-upon definition of an “emerging market,” and as such major financial institutions and fund managers usually just make up their own definitions based on certain characteristics of emerging markets. In particular, emerging markets usually have much stronger gross domestic product growth than developed markets, along with economies that are moving away from farming and towards manufacturing and technology development. However, even this definition is not precise, as MSCI still classifies South Korea as an emerging market despite the fact that many of the technological products used in the United States and the European Union are designed and manufactured there. Thus, it can be a matter of opinion which specific countries are considered to be emerging markets and, as such, which countries might be represented in this fund. However, potential investors can likely be certain that the United States, Canada, Japan, Australia, and most nations in the European Union will not have their debt securities included in this fund.

One thing that anyone reviewing the above list will likely notice is that the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is pretty well-diversified across countries. There are only six countries—Ecuador, India, Ivory Coast, Kazakhstan, Rwanda, and South Africa—that account for more than 5% of the fund’s net assets. If we include the Egyptian Treasury bills, then that country also accounts for more than 5% of the fund’s net assets, but as we can see here, those securities will all mature before the end of the year:

The most long-dated of the Egyptian Treasury bills matures in October, so the fund should not have its money tied up for too long in those securities. This is a nice characteristic for investors, as generally speaking, the closer to the present a debt security’s maturity date, the less likely it is to default. After all, there are fewer things that could potentially occur within the next few months than there are things that could potentially occur within the next ten years. As such, the default risk on these securities is probably not too high, but it may still be a good idea to be cautious. As the Harvard Kennedy School points out, Egypt has suffered eight balance of payment crises since 1952, with the most recent occurring in 2023 and 2024. Ultimately, the International Monetary Fund had to bail out the nation in early 2024. Today, the country is trying to return its economy to growth and reduce its external sovereign debt. The country has been seeing an increase in foreign investment lately, and naturally, it wants this trend to continue, as that is critical to the country being able to execute its economic development plan. The last thing that the government would want is to lose the confidence of foreign investors, and as such, it seems likely that the Egyptian government will do everything in its power to avoid defaulting on its short-term Treasury bills. As such, we can probably expect that the securities above will all be paid as agreed, and given the interest rate on them, the fund might stand to earn a very attractive profit as the bills mature.

One thing that we should note, however, is that the Egyptian Treasury bills are all denominated in Egyptian pounds. This means that the interest and face value to be paid to the fund at maturity will be in the form of that currency. This exposes the fund to currency risk, which could be problematic. As this chart shows, the Egyptian pound is down 8.20% against the U.S. dollar year-to-date:

This is not what we want to see when considering foreign currency-denominated bonds for purchase. This is because a decline in the EGP/USD exchange rate means that the Egyptian pounds that the fund receives at the maturity of each of the Treasury bills will convert into fewer U.S. dollars than was the case two months ago. In other words, the Egyptian Treasury bills are losing value in U.S. dollar terms. Obviously, this is not a good thing for investors in this fund. The fact that most of the Egyptian Treasury bills are paying 23% or higher interest rates should help to offset the losses from the currency exchange fluctuations, but it might still be a good idea to keep an eye on the exchange rate if you happen to be invested in this fund.

Al-Estiklal offers three explanations for the year-to-date decline in the Egyptian pound against the U.S. dollar. They mostly boil down to international investors attempting to reduce their exposure to what are perceived to be “risky assets,” particularly in the Middle East. It is very possible that the outbreak of hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran in the region has made investors nervous about having exposure to the area. While Egypt itself is not involved in this conflict, recent events have still made many participants in the international markets nervous, and, as such, they have begun moving their money out of emerging markets and back to developed markets. This trend is also evident in recent U.S. Treasury auctions, which have been showing signs of increasing appetites among international investors. For example, Zero Hedge points out that international buyers were fairly active in today’s ten-year U.S. Treasury auction, especially when compared to recent ones. We have also seen this in the stock market, which experienced a sell-off once the war broke out, as investors switched from risk assets (common equities) to cash. Currency valuations are highly affected by capital flows. As investors pull their money out of a country, they also sell the currency of that nation in order to convert it into the currency of a nation that they deem to be safer. That causes the currency of the country whose currency is being sold to decline against currencies such as the U.S. dollar. Unfortunately for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, it is difficult to see a situation where the Egyptian pound returns to its previous value before the hostilities in the Middle East end. There are a range of opinions on when that could be, but I would not be surprised if at least some of the fund’s Egyptian Treasury bills mature before investors become more confident about investing in Egypt and its currency regains the value that it had at the start of the year.

It is very possible that this weakness in the Egyptian currency has been a contributing factor in the fund’s poor performance since the start of this year:

Over the period from the start of the year through February 12, 2026, the fund’s share price was up 8.37%. As we can see here, it has fallen by 8.73% since that date:

Admittedly, the decline of the Egyptian pound against the U.S. dollar is not the only reason for the fund’s share price decline. After all, only 12.93% of the fund’s assets are denominated in Egyptian pounds:

There are other currencies that are more highly represented in this fund, including the South African rand at 15.40% of total assets. Thus, the fund does not really have excessive exposure to any foreign currency. In fact, its largest position is a negative 18.99% position in the U.S. dollar:

This could hurt the fund in a flight-to-safety situation, given that the U.S. dollar is the usual beneficiary in such situations. Basically, whenever uncertainty rattles the markets, investors tend to sell common stocks, emerging market bonds, emerging market currencies, and certain commodities. They use the proceeds of these sales to purchase U.S. Treasuries, U.S. dollars, and sometimes gold. This process tends to cause the U.S. dollar to rise against other currencies. In the case of this fund, that would be a negative, as the value of the fund’s long positions will decline when measured in U.S. dollars. It is likely that this is the biggest reason for the fund’s price decline over the past few weeks. The decline in the Egyptian pound to U.S. dollar exchange rate was not the sole cause.

It is important to watch currency markets with a fund like this due to the fact that bonds can be thought of as a promise to deliver a certain amount of a specified currency at a future date. An investor purchases a bond from the issuer at face value (or a discount to face value, in the case of a zero-coupon bond). The issuer then delivers a specified amount of fiat currency to the owner of the bond on a specified schedule and then pays the face amount of currency to the bondholder on the date that the bond matures. The actual value of these payments varies depending on the value of the currency. With a bond that is denominated in a currency other than the investor’s own, the payments (and the bond) increase in value when the currency in which the bond is denominated rises against the investor’s own currency and vice versa. As of the time of writing, all of the long positions of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund are denominated in a currency other than the U.S. dollar. As such, investors in this fund would be wise to pay at least some attention to the foreign exchange market, as the movements that occur there will have a major impact on the value of the fund’s assets.

Fortunately, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is diversified enough that it is unlikely that a movement in the value of a single currency will have an outsized impact on its portfolio. The sole exception here is a flight-to-safety situation, in which all currencies decline against the U.S. dollar. The fund is short U.S. dollars, so that would almost certainly cause its net asset value to decline. Fortunately, such situations tend not to be very long-lived, so any decline in the value of the bonds will likely be temporary.

Long-Term Outlook Looks Better

The long-term outlook for emerging market bonds, particularly local currency-denominated bonds such as the ones that comprise the portfolio of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, is quite good. This comes from the fact that it is very likely that emerging market currencies will rise against the U.S. dollar over the long term.

One of the biggest reasons for this is that the United States will likely have to increase its money supply substantially over the coming years. The Congressional Budget Office projects that the United States will have cumulative deficits of $24 trillion over the next ten years. The agency predicts a $1.9 trillion deficit for fiscal year 2026, which will grow to a $3.1 trillion deficit by 2036. For all ten years, the deficit is projected to exceed the 3.8% of gross domestic product that has been the average over the past five decades. In addition, the agency projects that the debt held by the public will reach 120% by 2036, which exceeds the 106% ratio that existed right at the end of World War 2. Furthermore, unlike the situation at the end of World War 2, the agency does not believe that the situation will get better after 2036. The long-term budget projections, which extend out to 2056, actually show the deficit getting even worse:

(Figures shown are as a percentage of gross domestic product.)

These figures assume that inflation will stay constant at 2% and that there will be no wars nor any changes to current law or entitlement programs that result in increased spending. None of these are realistic assumptions, but even with these assumptions, we can see that the federal deficit grows to 9.1% of U.S. gross domestic product and the public debt-to-GDP ratio increases to 175% by 2056.

It is exceedingly difficult to see any way in which the United States will be able to finance these deficits without the Federal Reserve essentially creating money to purchase debt securities. After all, the majority of the spending growth is due to increasing spending on Social Security and Medicare as the demographic trends in the United States point to the average age of the population increasing over the projection period. This will, over time, result in fewer people paying taxes into these programs and more people collecting from them. The traditional buyers of U.S. debt (namely Japan, China, and the European Union) all have the same demographic trends, and as such, the social programs in those nations will require more funding, leaving them less able to invest in American debt. They may even sell some of their American debt securities to finance their own domestic programs. In the end, this could easily force the Federal Reserve to buy U.S. Treasuries, which increases the money supply and contributes to inflation at home.

Many emerging markets do not have the same demographic concerns as the wealthy developed markets. They have younger populations than the United States or most of Europe. For example, as of 2025, the average age of the population in Egypt is 24.5 years, whereas the average age of the population of the United States surpassed 39 years in 2024. Furthermore, the fertility rate in Egypt is 2.92 children per woman compared to 1.79 in the United States. This points to the social programs in those countries (the ones that have them at least) not having the same problem as the ones in the developed world, as a greater proportion of the population will be working age for longer than in the United States, Japan, or the European Union. This should reduce the need for emerging market governments to run large deficits to support an aging population and support the valuation of their currencies versus the U.S. dollar.

Another factor to consider is that many emerging market nations are commodity exporters, and commodities tend to do fairly well in the face of monetary expansion. For example, the top-performing assets during the 1970s were gold, oil (and energy companies), mining companies, and other commodity producers. It is quite likely that these assets will do well again in an environment in which the Federal Reserve is forced to finance U.S. deficits. Currencies of emerging market nations usually benefit from rising commodity prices, which is at least partly because rising commodity prices tend to improve the balance of trade (exports minus imports) of commodity-producing emerging market nations.

These factors could make local currency emerging market bonds, such as the ones included in the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, good assets to hold over the coming years. Obviously, investors who own this fund would be beneficiaries as this thesis plays out.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0475 per share ($0.57 per share annually). This gives the fund an 8.93% yield at the current share price.

The Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund has released its annual report (linked earlier) for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, since our last discussion on it. Let us have a look at this document and see how well it managed to cover its distribution over the full-year 2025 period.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund reported a net investment income of $32,523,217. This was not sufficient to cover the $41,324,866 that the fund distributed over the same period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund reported net realized gains of $3,911,122 along with net unrealized gains of $57,562,814. Overall, the fund’s portfolio provided investment profits of $93,997,153 over the period:

This was sufficient to cover the distributions, but the fund had to rely on its net unrealized gains to do it. This could be concerning, given that unrealized gains are not necessarily permanent. The market can potentially erase unrealized gains in a correction or downturn, so we usually want to be cautious if the fund is relying on them to cover its distributions. Fortunately, though, in this case, there is probably not too much to worry about. The fund’s net investment income plus net realized gains was $36,434,339. That was sufficient to cover all except for $4,890,527 of the distribution. That was less than 10% of the fund’s net unrealized gains, so realistically, the market would have to move against the fund by quite a lot to erase the unrealized gains that it has used to partially offset the distribution. This is rather unlikely to occur, and we probably do not need to worry too much.

In conclusion, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is a closed-end fund that offers exposure to local currency emerging market bonds, which is an asset class that many investors do not have much exposure to. With that said, though, the conflict in Iran might put selling pressure on some of the bonds in the portfolio for the near term.
